body:
> they're not required to make the dropdown etc work correctly, if that was the question @thewebartisan7 ... and even for accessibility, they're more a "nice to have" than a hard requirement.

If it's optional, but "nice to have" that means it's _better_ to have it, right? So removing it in #36487 is therefore somewhat _worse_? As accessibility is a legal requirement for many of us, it would be nice if the examples reflected the accessible version. Maybe by "question of taste" you mean that it doesn't necessarily improve or reduce accessibility either way, however, I don't see anywhere in the changes an explanation (such as an example) that provides guidance. Based on the code changes, it seems like the recommendation is to remove these two attributes which are "confusing", though it's unclear to me if it's confusing for users of AT (which would be very bad, and it makes sense to fix the problem) or if it's confusing to developers (like me, who might end up at this discussion looking for an explanation).

I hope this helps clarify what this all looks like to users like me who are not in the loop. Thank you all for your amazing efforts to build the tools that make everything better.

body:
This is how I did it in Angular@16, with SASS and RTLCSS in package.json, before building the application:
1. build Bootstrap SCSS (with your customized variables), with sass, as /assets/bootstrap.min.css
2. build the RTL file with rtlcss, from /assets/bootstrap.min.css to /assets/bootstrap.rtl.min.css
3. in your index.html. link the /assets/bootstrap.rtl.min.css file, and set dir="rtl" and lang="ar" in the HTML element

So, my build script in package.json looks something like:
"build-all": "sass ... bootstrap.scss ./assets/bootstrap.min.css & rtlcss ... ./assets/bootstrap.min.css ./assets/bootstrap.rtl.min.css & ng build ...",
(the script paths depend on the bootstrap files location, and root options in angular.json, but you get the idea)

If you need to switch between LTR and RTL without reloading the page, you can update the DOM to change the link SRC and the dir/lang attributes
author: Filini

body:
As per the [Bootstrap docs](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/rtl/#get-familiar), you need a tool ([`rtlcss`](https://rtlcss.com/)) to process the generated css file from your scss project. Then `rtlcss`, in turn, generates an RTL css file.

1. Step One: compile sass to css (eg. `style.css`)
2. Step two: run `rtlcss` on `style.css` and generate `style.rtl.css`
3. Step three: manually change the stylesheet file on the document using javascript based on event

body:
Some bootstrap classes are still undercooked when it comes to `rtl`. 

i use `mx` tag instead of `ms` or `me` when it comes to multi language sites (that has both `ltr` and `rtl`). To a degree, it gives symmetry.

And yes default `ms` and `me` seems misleading when doing website in `rtl` format.

for now i use custom css like `[dir="rtl"] .ms-<X> {margin-right: <valueBasedonX>}`. Extra work but helps in avoiding confusion.

body:
Our implementation currently puts component-specific variables on the component level as to not flood the `:root` element. For example, if you're customizing your build by not importing half our components, we don't want those to still be there or require additional enabling/disabling on a per-component basis.

You will notice though in the `_root.scss` stylesheet that we have a growing number of global CSS variables that we reuse across the project for shared styles. Things like colors, fonts, borders, and more. Those are variables that can be overridden on the `:root` or `<body>` and have their changes globally inherited. This set of root variables will continue to grow with dark mode support coming in #35857 where we add additional, more nuanced shared colors (kind of like Apple's HIG).

With v5 at least, one of the main goals of adding CSS variables is to enable color mode support. But, it's not a feasible solution to have to customize every single component's colors to make that happen. As such, we've opted for fewer global variables and more component variables. This isn't a perfect setup yet—mixing Sass functionality with CSS variables is a little messy. However, this will continue to be iterated on and refined heading into a v6 eventually.
author: mdo

body:
Thanks for the explanation, @mdo. Not flooding `:root` with component-specific variables if those components aren’t being compiled into the final stylesheet makes sense.

Love the work that’s been done on this front so far, though. And keen to see where it heads in the future. Kudos to every one who’s been involved!

body:
I found this excellent tutorial, which uses using `display : grid` instead of `display : flex` to center. (https://chrisbracco.com/css-truly-center-a-single-child-element-horizontally-when-siblings-are-present/). The author explains the central issue, "The “centered” child element is only horizontally centered within the remaining space in the parent element, and not the “true center” of the parent element. In this case the width of the centered element is equal to the width of the parent element minus any sibling element(s). The centered element can only be “truly centered” if the width of child elements on both the left and right of it are equal." The tutorial solution boils down to this: ```
Center
``` The "solution" applied to my nav looks like the following below. Hopefully overriding `display: flex` in container-fluid, or any other navbar class I've used, with `display: grid` won't cause any issues. I haven't found any yet. BTW, I recommend that Boostrap provide a way to truly centring navbar components without the user having to add custom CSS. Please let me know if there is an easy way to do this with the current Bootstrap classes. I added the `truly-center-grid` to the container-fluid div. I didn't bother adding truly-center-child to the name-brand `` link or the navbar-nav `
    ` for the menu since specifying `grid-template-columns: 1fr auto 1fr` in the truly-center-grid class already places these components accordingly. At a later date, if I want a right-align search box, I can just add the bootstrap "text-end" class to the search form element/div. CSS ``` .truly-center-grid { display: grid !important; grid-template-columns: 1fr auto 1fr; align-items: center; } ``` HTML ```
    Samba du Soleil
    • Home
    • Band
    • Music
    • Events
    • More
      • Scores

body:
Hee there,

I've been using css grids for quite some time to build websites (creating fallbacks by first building the element using display block combined with floating, or just using display flex and putting the css grid code in the `@support(display:grid){}` feature query). A lot of browsers started to support css grids from 2017 onwards. Now that global support is 94.94% (!), and with Internet Explorer officially dead, I think it's time to go forward and implement css grids for Bootstrap as soon as possible. What are we waiting for?

And why the opt-in for css grids? If you don't want to use css grids, you don't put the d-grid class in your html, right?

For context, and to be clear, in Bootstrap you can use layouts in 3 ways:
1. Bootstrap grid system (container-row-col)
2. Flexbox
3. CSS Grid (opt-in)

An even more radical idea: I would propose to get rid of the Bootstrap grid system entirely, from let's say Bootstrap 6, and replace the container-row-col structure with a new Bootstrap grid system based on css grids, which does this in a cleaner way.

I find the Bootstrap grid system hacky because of the use of negative margins to create the column structure. When applying margins to columns, the negative margins (of these columns) is interfered with and the layout doesn't look like what you would expect. The same goes for applying padding to containers, which already have a default padding set to them.

Concerning the choice of flexbox vs css grids, I would always keep using both, for flexbox in my view, combines perfectly well together with css grids. It's a match made in heaven for us layout perfectionists.

To see the main discussion about implementing css grids, see #31813

body:
This is covered a bit in our docs: https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/customize/sass/#importing. If you include the whole of Bootstrap, you can place your variables first, but you may have issues with variable and map re-use later in our codebase. In addition, functions for things like SVGs and colors and more won't be available.

In contrast to importing individual pieces, you get full control and access to the functions. There are a few code comments that talk about this in the Customize> Sass page.

> // Include any default variable overrides here (though functions won't be available)

> 1. Include functions first (so you can manipulate colors, SVGs, calc, etc)

Modal title

The modal component being always open might have a fixed position on your screen (unless that's your intention). The card component can use header/footer like a modal. body:
Include the font however you like (Google Fonts has many options listed on their site), and then update the `font-family` wherever you need it. Usually it's enough to override that on the `<body>` element.

For example, add this to your HTML inside the `<head>`:

```html
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com"> 
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin> 
<link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Roboto&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
```

And in your CSS:

```css
body {
  font-family: 'Roboto', sans-serif;
}
```

body:
@qoietsammy it looks like you're using some templating engine (aspx?) to allow conditionals within your HTML. `<% if(campground.images.length > 1) %{>` When using any sort of templating engine (such as Jinja), something to bear in mind is that any component that uses an `id=""` attribute (in your case: `id="carouselExampleControls"`) must always be unique. IDs on an HTML page must always be unique, otherwise they won't work properly. It's quite possible that since you've got more than 1 carousel item to display, they're all "stacking" perhaps? Would you be able to provide a bit more context and code if that doesn't help? Tip: something I like to do when in any forloops with a templating engine to make my IDs unique is to incorporate the forloop counter into the ID. Example from Flask/Django (Jinja) syntax: ```jinja {% for foo in foobar %}
....
{% endfor %} ```

}

body:
@bubblypatrick 
Not entirely certain here, but something to consider is the fact that Bootstrap version 5 completely changes syntax from previous versions.

The newer version (5+) adds `-bs-` into the component data attributes, and it will not work with older versions. I see this daily with some junior developers that I mentor... Incorporating older CDN links, but grabbing version 5 components.

Perhaps food for thought on your issue, could have something to do with it...?

Example:

Version 3.4.x Modal
`data-toggle="modal"`

Version 4.6.x Modal
`data-toggle="modal"`

Version 5.2.x Modal
`data-bs-toggle="modal"`

Note: the `-bs-` (bootstrap) identifier in between.

body:
Yeah, but i mean, it doesn't completely solve the issue. There is still unused css moving around, regardless of whether or not it is being cached. I believe there should be a better way to deliver only requested css elements without the need to cache unwanted data. To do so, I think the second solution that i have already posted above somehow addressed the issue, but it would introduce a lot of `<link>` tag within the head element. Moreover, I am also thinking of building a cli that can accomplish this task. For instance, say you want to use the `btn` element; with such tool, namely `bootstrap-cli`, you can fetch the desired css elements like:

```sh
bootstrap-cli pull btn
``` 

Running the above command will fetch all the styling related to the `btn` and store it within a `style.css` file. This way, you are only using the required css elements, which results in much more efficient css file.

Regardless of which solution to use to solve this problem, i believe that having a large css file stored somewhere is a bit problematic, which results in lots of unwanted css declarations.

What do you think?

body: I am also experiencing the same - scrollspy is essentially unfunctional for a long single page website. The old behavior (bs4) would trigger scrollspy when the top of the targeted element crossed the top of the bounding element, and you could change the vertical offset. This worked perfectly for a long single page website. Now with intersection observer, the new behavior triggets scrollspy when a portion of the targeted element is visible. This isn't exact at all. Even Bootstrap docs examples are sloppy and imprecise. Intersection observer is great for some things but for scrollspy it's a nightmare!

body:
There are 2 key points you may want to understand:

1. Use a wrapper `<div>` to style you menu content. (Inside `.container-fluid` in my example). Let Bootstrap does its job for `<navbar>` and `<container-fluid>`. You manage your own styles using wrappers.
2. Use Bootstrap's Toggler system for navbar. Please see [Responsive behaviors - Navbar - Bootstrap Docs](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/components/navbar/#responsive-behaviors). It contains a `<button>` as toggler and a `<div>` as a list of menu. Please make sure you use a good "id" to toggler only one element.

I created a demo for you: https://codepen.io/Edditoria/pen/NWMpmdJ

And, I added a `<div.navbar-toggler-wrapper>` for better style for the hamburger menu. It is not necessary but only "not going to hurt your eyes". 😸 

---

Bootstrap is highly rely on breakpoints for screen (media queries), rather than contents (element queries). And make sure it display properly on modern browsers, without or with-less Javascript.

For your case, you will need Javascript to get the width of the whole navbar. It can only be done **after** the browser renders the page (that users may use vary fonts, scales and other settings). I think Bootstrap does not have this feature.

Thanks.

Consume the files in https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/tree/main/js/dist ?

Or, in your JS code, `import { Collapse } from 'bootstrap/dist/js/bootstrap/bootstrap.esm.js';`?

As told in Bootstrap [docs][docs], it is quite complicated if you do it yourself. And, since you are developing website, I would use a bundler to resolve NPM packages, tree-shaking and distribute JS files for browser compatibility , e.g. Rollup, Webpack. Example:

```js
import { Collapse } from 'bootstrap'; // Type of Collapse is a class.
// You code...

// Or simply
export default Collapse
```

[docs]: https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/getting-started/javascript/#using-bootstrap-as-a-module

Knowing that AdminLTE provides a nice tested solution with plenty of component choices, and 'plain' BS provides a solid base and some standard components, it comes the moment that you, and only you, can take this decision, consider it in combination of your UI needs and the tools you use.

I was on the same thoughts some years ago, but in my case I was using a php template engine that was supporting php-components usage (easy and in one place, maintained blocks & markup), and the frontend wasn't too demanding.  At that time, using webpack lazy loading for my assets, avoiding keeping a js-vendor folder full of scripts, and use only one or two script tags in my code, seemed probably a better solution for me.  

3 years later, I haven't regretted my decision, as I kept the dependencies to the minimum, but yes, there have been times during js refactoring and UI addons, where I needed to implement some minor stuff myself

.header-title {

Include it in your HTML after Bootstrap's CSS:

```html
<link rel="stylesheet" href="path/to/bootstrap.css">
<link rel="stylesheet" href="path/to/custom.css">
```

My 2 cents. Few elements that could help figuring out what to do next.

### Option 1: Update `.browserlistrc`

<details>
  <summary>Current browser support</summary>
npx autoprefixer --info                                                                                                           (0.000001 hrs)

Browsers:
  Chrome for Android: 105
  Firefox for Android: 104
  QQ Browser: 13.1

  iOS Safari: 16.0, 15.6, 15.5, 15.4, 15.2-15.3, 15.0-15.1, 14.5-14.8, 14.0-14.4, 13.4-13.7, 13.3, 13.2, 13.0-13.1, 12.2-12.5, 12.0-12.1

  Opera Mobile: 64
  Opera: 91, 90, 89
  Safari: 16.0, 15.6, 15.5, 15.4, 15.2-15.3, 15.1, 15, 14.1, 14, 13.1, 13, 12.1, 12
  Samsung Internet: 18.0, 17.0

These browsers account for 95.97% of all users globally
</details>

By updating to `Chrome >= 86` + `Firefox >= 85` (and letting Firefox ESR) we got

<details>
  <summary>Updated .browserlistrc</summary>
Browsers:
  Chrome for Android: 105
  Firefox for Android: 104
  QQ Browser: 13.1
  UC for Android: 13.4
  Android: 105
  Chrome: 106, 105, 104, 103, 102, 101, 100, 99, 98, 97, 96, 95, 94, 93, 92, 91, 90, 89, 88, 87, 86
  Edge: 105, 104, 103
  Firefox: 105, 104, 103, 102, 101, 100, 99, 98, 97, 96, 95, 94, 93, 92, 91, 90, 89, 88, 87, 86, 85
  iOS Safari: 16.0, 15.6, 15.5, 15.4, 15.2-15.3, 15.0-15.1, 14.5-14.8, 14.0-14.4, 13.4-13.7, 13.3, 13.2, 13.0-13.1, 12.2-12.5, 12.0-12.1

  Safari: 16.0, 15.6, 15.5, 15.4, 15.2-15.3, 15.1, 15, 14.1, 14, 13.1, 13, 12.1, 12
  Samsung Internet: 18.0, 17.0

These browsers account for 94.91% of all users globally
</details>

So we'd loose 1.06% of all users globally.
I haven't done the exercise but maybe some other browsers versions could need an update in this file as well (it would increase this number obviously).
I don't have the answer but I'm raising the question: would it be "OK"?

### If we update the file, when do we do it?

IMO updating this file shouldn't be in a dot version of Bootstrap.
Correct me if I'm wrong but we plan to use `prefers-color-scheme` for the dark mode support in v5.3.0 that seems to need at least Firefox 67 and Chrome 76 (source: https://caniuse.com/?search=prefers-color-scheme). So we'd need here an update of this `.browserlistrc` as well.

Extra-question here. If we don't update the file right now, do we need to rollback the modifications already merged for `:focus-visible`? (might be not so simple)

### Option 2: `focus-visible` polyfill

If we consider that this `.browserlistrc` should remain the same for now, another option is to use the `focus-visible` polyfill: https://github.com/WICG/focus-visible (that's what we use in Boosted actually). The drawbacks for this solution are the following:
* this polyfill is always enabled even if the user's browser support `:focus-visible`
* the syntax is a bit different than `:focus-visible` (see https://github.com/WICG/focus-visible#2-update-your-css)

body:
OK so you have this rule in __reboot.scss_: `text-align: var(--#{$prefix}body-text-align);` (`$prefix` is set to `bs-` in the project).
This rule is in `body {`.
So, in your browser (or in a CSS file in your project or with inline CSS with `<body style="--bs-body-text-align: end;">` for example) you can do stuff like that `--bs-body-text-align: end;` for example and the body text will be aligned on the right in LTR mode.

Our doc is obviously using Bootstrap so here is the rendering when I change this value dynamically in the browser:

![Screenshot 2022-10-22 at 10 24 48](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17381666/197328991-0a5ca663-b459-4fa0-a76a-c58f65b7daf2.png)

More info in [Content > Reboot > CSS variables](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/content/reboot/#css-variables)
